Original_FR: For each class within a sequence for a cohort Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members shall be able to specify which quarter that class will be offered.
Conflicting_FR: Only Program Administrators shall be able to specify the quarter; Nursing Staff Members shall not have permission to define class quarters.
Resolution:
1. **Restrict quarter assignment authority to Program Administrators only**: Modify Requirement A to remove Nursing Staff Members from the list of authorized agents, aligning it with Requirement B. The revised requirement would state: “For each class within a sequence for a cohort, Program Administrators shall be able to specify which quarter that class will be offered.” This eliminates the agent conflict by clearly assigning the responsibility to a single role.

2. **Introduce a collaborative workflow with role-based permissions**: Allow Nursing Staff Members to propose or suggest quarters (e.g., via a recommendation interface), but require final approval and official assignment by Program Administrators. This preserves input from Nursing Staff while ensuring that only Program Administrators have the authoritative permission to set the quarter, satisfying both requirements through differentiated agent roles.

3. **Define context-specific permissions based on cohort or program type**: Specify that in certain programs or cohorts (e.g., those led by nursing departments), Nursing Staff Members may be granted quarter-assignment rights, while in others, only Program Administrators retain this ability. This resolves the conflict by scoping agent permissions conditionally, provided such delegation rules are explicitly defined in system policy or configuration.

Original_FR: For each shot the product shall examine the defensive grid of the defensive player to determine whether the shot was a hit or miss.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not examine the defensive grid before determining whether the shot was a hit or miss.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify temporal scope in Requirement B**: Revise Requirement B to specify that the product shall not examine *any part of the defensive grid other than the targeted cell* before determining hit/miss. This preserves the intent of Requirement A (examining the relevant grid cell for adjudication) while honoring Requirement B’s goal of avoiding premature or unnecessary access to the full defensive grid.

2. **Refine input trigger definition**: Modify both requirements to explicitly state that “examination of the defensive grid” occurs *only after* a shot coordinate is fully received and validated as input. This ensures that grid examination is tightly coupled to the shot event itself, eliminating ambiguity about when the input (shot location) is considered available for processing.

3. **Introduce an intermediate adjudication step**: Add a system-level specification that decouples “receiving the shot” from “accessing the grid.” Define that the system first logs the shot input, then—only as part of the hit/miss determination step—queries the specific grid cell. This satisfies Requirement A’s need to examine the grid for adjudication while ensuring Requirement B’s constraint (no pre-determination grid access) is met by design.

Original_FR: For each shot the product shall mark the shot as a hit or miss on the offensive grid of the offensive player.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not display offensive shot results to maintain player privacy.
Resolution:
1. **Modify Requirement A to limit visibility**: Revise Requirement A to specify that shot results (hit/miss) are marked only on the offensive player’s *private* offensive grid view and are never visible to the opposing player or any shared/public display, thereby satisfying Requirement B’s privacy restriction.

2. **Introduce conditional display logic**: Clarify that the product shall mark shot results internally for game logic purposes (e.g., determining win conditions), but shall suppress visual rendering of those results on any interface accessible to other players—effectively decoupling data recording from display as allowed by Requirement B.

3. **Refine terminology and scope in both requirements**: Update Requirement A to state that marking occurs “for internal tracking and the offensive player’s own view only,” and amend Requirement B to explicitly permit self-viewing by the offensive player while prohibiting disclosure to others, eliminating ambiguity and aligning the two requirements.

Original_FR: For each shot the product shall notify the players whether the shot was a hit or miss.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not notify the players immediately; notifications will only be sent after the end of the turn.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to specify that hit/miss notifications are generated per shot but deferred until the end of the turn, aligning with Requirement B’s timing constraint (e.g., “For each shot, the product shall record whether it was a hit or miss and notify players of all such results only after the end of the turn.”).

2. Introduce a conditional clause in Requirement B to allow immediate notifications for certain game modes or player roles while preserving delayed notifications as the default (e.g., “Unless configured otherwise for specific gameplay variants, the product shall not notify players immediately…”), thereby accommodating both requirements through configurability.

3. Clarify the definition of “turn” in Requirement B to ensure it encompasses individual shots in single-shot turns, effectively making “end of the turn” equivalent to “immediately after the shot” in those contexts—resolving the apparent conflict by harmonizing event timing semantics.

Original_FR: For each turn the product shall allow the offensive player to define a shot.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall automatically define the offensive player's shot without user input.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a configurable mode**: Modify both requirements to specify that the system shall support two distinct operational modes—manual and automatic. In manual mode, Requirement A applies (user defines the shot); in automatic mode, Requirement B applies (system auto-defines the shot). The product shall allow the user to select the mode at the start of each turn or session.

2. **Define precedence with fallback behavior**: Clarify that Requirement A takes precedence by default (user defines the shot), but if no user input is received within a specified time window, the system shall automatically define the shot per Requirement B. This resolves the conflict by sequencing the operations and defining clear triggering conditions.

3. **Refine scope by player type or context**: Specify that Requirement A applies when the offensive player is human, while Requirement B applies only when the offensive player is an AI-controlled entity. This eliminates the operational overlap by tying each requirement to a distinct user context.

Original_FR: For each turn the product shall identify one player as offensive status and one player as defensive status.
Conflicting_FR: Both players shall be assigned offensive and defensive status simultaneously.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify temporal scope in Requirement A**: Modify Requirement A to specify that the assignment of one offensive and one defensive player applies *per turn phase* (e.g., during a specific sub-phase like “attack resolution”), while allowing both players to hold dual roles at other times. This reconciles the restriction by limiting its applicability to a defined interval within the turn.

2. **Refine role semantics in Requirement B**: Revise Requirement B to state that both players are *eligible* for both offensive and defensive status simultaneously, but only one of each is *active* per turn as required by Requirement A. This preserves the intent of simultaneous eligibility while respecting the exclusive assignment constraint per turn.

3. **Introduce hierarchical role assignment**: Define a two-layer status model where a “primary” offensive/defensive role is assigned per turn (satisfying Requirement A), while “secondary” or “potential” roles may be held by both players concurrently (satisfying Requirement B). Explicitly distinguish between active and latent statuses in the specification to eliminate ambiguity.

Original_FR: For totally unstructured information, AI libraries will be used to programmatically extract and store any information possible
Conflicting_FR: For totally unstructured information, manual extraction shall be used to ensure accuracy.
Resolution:
1. **Define a tiered processing workflow**: Modify both requirements to specify that AI-based extraction (Requirement A) is applied first as an initial pass on totally unstructured information, followed by manual verification or correction (Requirement B) only for high-priority or low-confidence results. This coordinates the two operations sequentially rather than treating them as mutually exclusive.

2. **Refine scope by data criticality**: Clarify Requirement A to apply to non-critical or bulk unstructured data where approximate extraction suffices, and refine Requirement B to apply exclusively to mission-critical or regulatory unstructured data where accuracy is paramount. This partitions the operational domain to eliminate overlap.

3. **Introduce a hybrid extraction mode**: Replace both requirements with a single unified requirement stating that “For totally unstructured information, a hybrid approach combining AI-assisted extraction with human-in-the-loop validation shall be used,” specifying thresholds (e.g., confidence scores) that trigger manual review. This resolves the conflict by integrating both operations into a cohesive process.

Original_FR: Formula substitutionary ingredients shall be kept in persisted order
Conflicting_FR: Substitutionary ingredients may be reordered automatically by the system for optimization.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a configurable persistence mode**: Modify Requirement A to state that “Formula substitutionary ingredients shall be kept in persisted order *unless explicitly overridden by an optimization policy*,” and refine Requirement B to specify that automatic reordering occurs only when a system-configurable optimization flag is enabled. This creates a conditional restriction that respects both persistence and optimization needs.

2. **Define separate contexts for ordering**: Clarify that Requirement A applies to the *authoring or archival view* of the formula (where user-intended order must be preserved), while Requirement B applies only to the *runtime or execution context* (where the system may reorder for performance or cost). This resolves the conflict by scoping each requirement to a distinct operational context.

3. **Implement versioned ingredient lists**: Allow the system to maintain the original persisted order as the authoritative version (satisfying Requirement A) while generating and storing optimized, reordered variants as derived artifacts (satisfying Requirement B). The specification should mandate that any automatic reordering produces a new version or variant, never altering the original persisted sequence.

Original_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, the first lines are shown.
Conflicting_FR: From the full text of the news articles, the first paragraphs are shown.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify the source input for both requirements by defining a unified content hierarchy**: Specify that “first titles” (Requirement A) and “full text” (Requirement B) refer to distinct, non-overlapping inputs—for example, titles are drawn from metadata fields, while full text comes from the article body. This eliminates ambiguity about which input each requirement operates on.

2. **Introduce a precedence or context rule**: Define that Requirement A applies only when the system is in a “headline-only” view mode (e.g., a summary feed), whereas Requirement B applies in a “preview” or “expanded” view mode. This contextual separation ensures both requirements can coexist without conflicting over the same input stream.

3. **Standardize the input schema to include both title and body as separate, labeled fields**: Require that all news article inputs conform to a structured format (e.g., JSON with "title" and "body" fields). Then explicitly state that Requirement A uses the "title" field and Requirement B uses the "body" field, removing any ambiguity about what constitutes the “input” for each.

Original_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, the first lines are shown.
Conflicting_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, no preview lines shall be shown to minimize clutter.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a user-configurable display setting that allows users to toggle preview lines on or off, satisfying Requirement A by default (showing first lines) while enabling compliance with Requirement B for users who prefer minimal clutter.  
2. Define context-specific behavior: show preview lines only in expanded or detailed views (e.g., when a title is hovered or selected), while keeping the primary list view free of previews—thus meeting both requirements through conditional output.  
3. Refine both requirements by specifying that “first lines” in Requirement A refer exclusively to metadata (e.g., publication timestamp or source) rather than article content, ensuring no textual preview is displayed and aligning with Requirement B’s intent to avoid clutter.

Original_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, the first lines are shown.
Conflicting_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, no preview lines shall be shown.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a user-configurable display setting**: Modify both requirements to specify that preview lines from article titles are shown only when the user enables a “Show Previews” option in their preferences. This resolves the conflict by making the output conditional on user choice, satisfying both the desire for previews (Requirement A) and the need to suppress them by default or on demand (Requirement B).

2. **Define context-specific output behavior**: Clarify that preview lines are shown in certain views (e.g., search results or personalized feeds per Requirement A) but explicitly suppressed in other contexts (e.g., compact lists or notification panels per Requirement B). This partitions the output behavior by interface context, eliminating direct contradiction.

3. **Refine “preview lines” definition and scope**: Specify that Requirement A refers only to displaying the article title itself (i.e., the first line *is* the title), while Requirement B prohibits showing additional summary text beyond the title. By aligning both requirements to treat the title as the sole permissible “first line,” the apparent conflict is resolved through precise terminology.

Original_FR: From the here the user can either proceed to call a rep for an order or proceed to the online checkout process
Conflicting_FR: From here, the user shall only be allowed to proceed to online checkout; calling a rep is disabled.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a user role or eligibility condition**: Modify both requirements to specify that the ability to call a rep is restricted to certain user types (e.g., premium customers or business accounts), while all other users are limited to online checkout only. This resolves the conflict by contextualizing Requirement A and aligning it with the restriction in Requirement B.

2. **Define a mutually exclusive workflow branch based on order characteristics**: Clarify that if an order meets specific criteria (e.g., high value, complex configuration, or international shipping), the “call a rep” option is available (per Requirement A); otherwise, only online checkout is permitted (per Requirement B). This turns the apparent conflict into a conditional business rule.

3. **Refine the UI state definition to separate contexts**: Specify that Requirement A applies on one screen/state (e.g., product summary page), while Requirement B applies on a subsequent, more constrained screen (e.g., final checkout initiation page). This eliminates the conflict by ensuring the two requirements govern different system states rather than the same decision point.

Original_FR: From the here the user can either proceed to call a rep for an order or proceed to the online checkout process.
Conflicting_FR: From here, users shall only be allowed to call a rep; online checkout is disabled.
Resolution:
1. **Refine Requirement A with a conditional clause**: Modify Requirement A to specify that the online checkout option is available only under certain conditions (e.g., user role, order type, or business hours), while Requirement B applies in contexts where those conditions are not met. For example: “From here, the user can proceed to call a rep for an order or, if eligible, proceed to the online checkout process.”

2. **Introduce user segmentation or context-based routing**: Clarify that Requirement B applies to a specific user segment (e.g., enterprise customers) or scenario (e.g., high-value orders), while Requirement A applies to general users. Update both requirements to explicitly reference their applicable user types or contexts to eliminate overlap.

3. **Establish precedence through requirement prioritization**: Designate Requirement B as a higher-priority rule that overrides Requirement A in all cases, and revise Requirement A accordingly to remove the online checkout path where Requirement B is active. Document this precedence in the system specification to ensure consistent implementation.

Original_FR: Full information for registration Given the restaurant owner wants to create an account And the restaurant owner does not have an account When the restaurant owner registers on the web-portal by providing user name And password And address And e-mail address And phone number And mobile number Then the restaurant owner should be able to apply for verification
Conflicting_FR: If the restaurant owner selects the registration option, the system shall create a temporary account requiring only a user name and password, and defer the collection of address, e-mail, phone number, and mobile number until the verification application is initiated.
Resolution:
1. **Adopt a phased input model**: Modify Requirement A to align with Requirement B by splitting registration into two distinct phases—(1) initial account creation (requiring only username and password) and (2) verification application (requiring full details). This resolves the input conflict by clarifying that “registration” in Requirement A refers to the complete onboarding process, not a single-step input.

2. **Clarify terminology and scope**: Explicitly redefine “registers on the web-portal” in Requirement A to mean “initiates the registration process,” while specifying that full information is only required at the point of verification application. This eliminates ambiguity about when each set of inputs is collected, harmonizing both requirements.

3. **Introduce conditional input validation**: Update the system specification to conditionally require fields based on user action—i.e., only username and password are mandatory for temporary account creation, but all fields (address, email, phone, mobile) become mandatory when the user triggers the “apply for verification” action. This satisfies both requirements by making input requirements context-dependent.

Original_FR: Full information for registration Given the restaurant owner wants to create an account And the restaurant owner does not have an account When the restaurant owner registers on the web-portal by providing user name And password And address And e-mail address And phone number And mobile number Then the restaurant owner should be able to apply for verification
Conflicting_FR: The restaurant owner must provide verification before registration is allowed; unverified accounts cannot be created.
Resolution:
1. **Reorder the registration and verification steps**: Modify Requirement A so that account creation is provisional—allow the restaurant owner to submit registration data (username, password, contact details, etc.), but mark the account as “pending verification.” Only after successful verification (as required by Requirement B) is the account fully activated. This aligns the event sequence: data submission → verification → account activation.

2. **Clarify “registration” terminology**: Explicitly distinguish between “submitting registration information” and “completing registration.” Update both requirements to state that Requirement A describes the submission of data, while Requirement B governs the finalization of registration. This eliminates the apparent conflict by decoupling data entry from account approval as separate events.

3. **Introduce a two-phase event flow**: Define a new system behavior where the initial event (from Requirement A) triggers a verification request (e.g., email/SMS confirmation or document upload), and only upon completion of that verification event (per Requirement B) does the system finalize registration. This ensures both requirements are satisfied in sequence without logical contradiction.

Original_FR: Full news report is shown after choosing the corresponding news title.
Conflicting_FR: Full news reports shall only display summaries instead of the entire content.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a user-controlled toggle**: Modify both requirements to allow the full news report to be displayed by default (satisfying Requirement A), but include a user-configurable setting or UI toggle that switches the display to summary-only mode (satisfying Requirement B). This resolves the output conflict by making the output behavior context-dependent on user preference rather than mutually exclusive.

2. **Differentiate by user role or access level**: Clarify that Requirement A applies to authenticated or premium users who are permitted to view full reports, while Requirement B applies to anonymous or standard users who only see summaries. This partitions the output behavior based on user type, eliminating direct conflict while preserving both intents.

3. **Define a staged disclosure model**: Revise the requirements so that selecting a news title initially displays a summary (aligning with Requirement B), and the full report is revealed only upon an explicit secondary action (e.g., clicking “Read Full Article”). This satisfies both requirements by sequencing the outputs—summary first, full content optionally—thereby resolving the output conflict through progressive disclosure.

Original_FR: Full news report is shown after choosing the corresponding news title.
Conflicting_FR: Only a summary of the news report shall be displayed instead of the full content.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a two-step display mechanism**: Modify the system behavior so that selecting a news title initially displays only the summary (satisfying Requirement B), followed by an explicit user action (e.g., “Read Full Article” button) to reveal the full news report (satisfying Requirement A). This resolves the conflict by sequencing the outputs based on user intent.

2. **Differentiate output based on user role or preference**: Clarify the requirements by specifying that the default view shows a summary (per Requirement B), but authenticated users or those with specific preferences enabled see the full report immediately upon selection (per Requirement A). This tailors the output to context without violating either requirement.

3. **Define distinct UI contexts for each output**: Specify that in list or feed views, only summaries are shown (Requirement B), while navigating to a dedicated article page (via title selection) displays the full report (Requirement A). This spatial separation ensures both requirements apply to different output contexts, eliminating direct conflict.

Original_FR: Functionalities related to Electronic Programming Guide and DRM management shall be implemented in STB or media gateway and not in CNG.
Conflicting_FR: Functionalities related to EPG and DRM shall be implemented exclusively in CNG, not in STB or media gateway.
Resolution:
1. **Reconcile implementation scope by partitioning EPG/DRM functionalities**: Clarify that core EPG rendering and user interaction logic reside in the CNG (per Requirement B), while low-level DRM license handling and conditional access remain in the STB/media gateway (per Requirement A). Explicitly define which sub-functions belong to each component in a shared interface specification.

2. **Introduce a conditional clause based on deployment architecture**: Amend both requirements to include a conditional statement such as: “If the system architecture includes a CNG with sufficient processing and security capabilities, EPG and DRM functionalities shall be implemented exclusively in the CNG; otherwise, they shall be implemented in the STB or media gateway.” This resolves the conflict by making implementation location dependent on validated system configurations.

3. **Establish a single source of truth via updated system architecture documentation**: Require alignment with an authoritative system architecture document that explicitly assigns EPG and DRM responsibilities to either CNG or STB/media gateway. Both Requirement A and B should be revised to reference this document, ensuring consistency and eliminating contradictory mandates.

Original_FR: Generate_H/C_Request shall include the designation of the heating or cooling unit and a flag to indicate whether that unit is to be turned on or off.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_H/C_Request shall exclude the flag indicating ON/OFF status and only include unit designation.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a configurable parameter**: Modify the specification to define Generate_H/C_Request as having a configurable output format—e.g., via a system configuration flag or mode setting—where the inclusion of the ON/OFF flag is optional based on operational context (e.g., “basic” vs. “extended” request mode). This allows both requirements to be satisfied in different deployment scenarios without contradiction.

2. **Refine requirement scope by use case**: Clarify that Requirement A applies to interactive or manual control requests (where explicit ON/OFF intent is needed), while Requirement B applies to automated or scheduled requests (where unit activation is implied by presence in the request). Document this distinction explicitly in the requirements specification to eliminate ambiguity.

3. **Adopt a layered message structure**: Redesign Generate_H/C_Request to always include the unit designation as the base payload, and make the ON/OFF flag an optional field (e.g., using nullable or optional data types). Update both requirements to reference this unified schema, with Requirement A mandating the optional field be populated, and Requirement B allowing it to be omitted—resolving the conflict through explicit optionality rather than mutual exclusion.

Original_FR: Generate_H/C_Signal shall recognize the values for the requested heating or cooling unit and the ON or OFF status that is being requested. Since the interface to control the units has not been defined, the part of this process that will issue the signal is designated as TBD (to be determined).
Conflicting_FR: Generate_H/C_Signal shall not process any values until the interface is fully defined; signals shall not be issued for undefined units.
Resolution:
1. **Refine Requirement A to defer signal generation logic until interface definition**: Modify Requirement A to state that while the *recognition* of heating/cooling unit values and ON/OFF status may be implemented, the actual *issuance* of control signals is explicitly prohibited until the interface is formally defined—aligning with Requirement B’s restriction. This preserves early validation of input parsing without violating the restriction on signal output.

2. **Introduce a conditional clause in both requirements based on interface maturity**: Add a shared condition (e.g., “if and only if the H/C unit interface specification is approved and baselined”) to both requirements. Requirement A would then only apply post-interface definition, eliminating the conflict by making signal generation contingent on the same prerequisite that Requirement B enforces.

3. **Separate recognition from actuation in system architecture**: Update the specification to split Generate_H/C_Signal into two distinct sub-functions: (a) *Parse_H/C_Request* (allowed per Requirement A, even pre-interface) and (b) *Issue_H/C_Signal* (explicitly gated by interface availability per Requirement B). This architectural clarification resolves the restriction conflict by isolating the restricted behavior (signal issuance) from the unrestricted one (value recognition).

Original_FR: geo-location feature to be able to find a user
Conflicting_FR: The geo-location feature shall not track any user location to protect privacy.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce user-consented, on-demand location sharing**: Modify Requirement A to specify that the geo-location feature only activates when explicitly authorized by the user for a specific purpose (e.g., “find a friend” or emergency assistance). This aligns with Requirement B by ensuring no passive or continuous tracking occurs without consent.

2. **Implement ephemeral, non-storable location data**: Clarify that any location data retrieved under Requirement A is processed in real time and immediately discarded after use, with no storage or logging permitted. This satisfies Requirement B’s privacy goal while still enabling the core functionality of locating a user when needed.

3. **Decouple identification from location data**: Redesign the system so that the geo-location feature returns anonymized or pseudonymized coordinates (e.g., relative proximity or zone-based location) rather than precise, personally identifiable location tied to a user identity. This allows “finding” functionality without violating the no-tracking principle in Requirement B.

Original_FR: GPS Connection DESC: The application should be connected to the GPS device.
Conflicting_FR: The application shall function without GPS connection; GPS is optional.
Resolution:
1. Refine Requirement A to specify that GPS connection is required only for GPS-dependent features (e.g., “The application shall connect to the GPS device when location-based functionality is enabled”), thereby aligning with Requirement B’s assertion that GPS is optional for overall system operation.

2. Introduce a conditional input specification: define two operational modes—“GPS-enabled mode” and “GPS-disabled mode”—and explicitly state which inputs (e.g., real-time location data) are expected in each mode, ensuring the system gracefully handles absence of GPS input without failure.

3. Add an input validation and fallback clause: require the system to detect GPS availability at startup or runtime and, if unavailable, disable or substitute GPS-dependent functions with alternative inputs (e.g., manual location entry), thus satisfying both the desire for GPS integration and the need for optional use.

Original_FR: Icons are according to the Android Icon Guidelines.
Conflicting_FR: Icons shall be customized arbitrarily and not follow Android Icon Guidelines.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a conditional compliance rule**: Modify Requirement A to state, “Icons shall follow the Android Icon Guidelines unless explicitly overridden by user customization settings,” and revise Requirement B to specify, “User-customized icons may deviate from Android Icon Guidelines but must not interfere with system-level icon recognition or accessibility features.” This creates a clear hierarchy where guidelines apply by default, but customization is permitted within safe boundaries.

2. **Define scope separation**: Clarify that Requirement A applies only to system-provided or default application icons (e.g., those used in launcher or system UI), while Requirement B applies exclusively to user-uploaded or third-party theme icons. This eliminates overlap by assigning each requirement to a distinct icon category, resolving the restriction conflict through domain partitioning.

3. **Adopt a hybrid design standard**: Update both requirements to align with a new, unified specification: “All icons—whether default or customized—must adhere to core Android Icon Guidelines for size, touch target, and contrast (to ensure usability), but may vary freely in visual style (e.g., color, shape, metaphor).” This preserves platform consistency where it matters most while allowing aesthetic customization, directly addressing the restriction conflict by narrowing the scope of mandatory compliance.

Original_FR: If a client registers for notifications, the UIMiddleware shall send notifications for all events.
Conflicting_FR: The UIMiddleware shall send notifications only for critical events; all other events are ignored.
Resolution:
1. **Refine Requirement A with a client-configurable scope**: Modify Requirement A to state, “If a client registers for notifications, the UIMiddleware shall send notifications according to the event scope selected by the client (e.g., all events or only critical events).” This resolves the restriction conflict by allowing the client’s registration to include a preference that aligns with Requirement B’s constraint.

2. **Introduce role- or policy-based notification rules**: Clarify that Requirement B applies by default, but Requirement A applies only to privileged clients (e.g., administrators). Update both requirements to reflect that “The UIMiddleware shall send notifications only for critical events to standard clients, but may send all events to clients with elevated permissions,” thereby reconciling the restrictions through access control.

3. **Define “notifications” contextually per requirement**: Disambiguate the term “notifications” by specifying that Requirement A refers to a broad logging or audit channel, while Requirement B governs user-facing alerts. Revise the requirements to use distinct terms (e.g., “event logs” vs. “user alerts”) so each applies to a different communication mechanism, eliminating the apparent restriction conflict.

Original_FR: If a client registers for notifications, the UIMiddleware shall send notifications for specific events.
Conflicting_FR: The UIMiddleware shall send notifications for all events regardless of registration preferences.
Resolution:
1. **Refine Requirement B to exclude registered clients**: Modify Requirement B to state that “The UIMiddleware shall send notifications for all events to clients who have not registered a preference,” thereby restricting its scope to unregistered or default users, while allowing Requirement A to govern behavior for registered clients.

2. **Introduce a precedence rule**: Clarify in the specification that client registration preferences (Requirement A) take precedence over the default notification policy (Requirement B). This resolves the restriction conflict by explicitly defining that user-configured settings override system-wide defaults.

3. **Differentiate event types**: Amend both requirements to distinguish between “critical” and “non-critical” events—e.g., Requirement B applies only to critical events (sent to all users regardless of preference), while Requirement A governs non-critical events (sent only upon registration). This partitions the scope to eliminate overlap and conflicting mandates.

Original_FR: If a command is issued by the VaadinUI for an UAV, the VaadinUI shall request control of that UAV.
Conflicting_FR: VaadinUI shall not request control of the UAV; commands are executed automatically by the UAV system.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify the control delegation model**: Modify Requirement A to specify that the VaadinUI only requests *logical* or *temporary* control (e.g., for command validation or session-based interaction) while Requirement B remains valid for autonomous execution. Explicitly define that “requesting control” does not override the UAV’s autonomous command execution but rather registers intent for audit or coordination purposes.

2. **Introduce a conditional trigger**: Revise both requirements to include a condition under which control is requested. For example: “If a command requires human-in-the-loop confirmation or overrides autonomous behavior, the VaadinUI shall request control of the UAV; otherwise, commands are executed automatically by the UAV system.” This aligns both requirements under distinct operational modes.

3. **Refactor roles in the command flow**: Redefine Requirement A to state that the VaadinUI *notifies* the UAV control manager of an intended command, and the control manager—not the UI—decides whether to grant control based on system policy. Requirement B is then preserved as the default behavior, with control requests handled by a dedicated subsystem rather than the UI directly.

Original_FR: If a flight plan is active and the "hover in place" command is rescinded, the FlightManager shall notify the UAV to continue to the originally designated waypoint.
Conflicting_FR: If the "hover in place" command is rescinded, the UAV shall remain hovering until manual override is performed.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a mode-specific behavior clause**: Modify both requirements to specify that the UAV’s response to rescinding the "hover in place" command depends on whether a flight plan is active. For example: “If a flight plan is active and the 'hover in place' command is rescinded, the FlightManager shall notify the UAV to continue to the originally designated waypoint. If no flight plan is active, the UAV shall remain hovering until manual override is performed.” This resolves the event conflict by conditioning the outcome on system state.

2. **Define priority and authority of control sources**: Clarify that the FlightManager has higher authority than local UAV hover logic when a flight plan is active. Update Requirement B to include an exception: “...unless a valid active flight plan exists, in which case the UAV shall follow instructions from the FlightManager.” This ensures consistent behavior based on control hierarchy rather than ambiguous parallel commands.

3. **Standardize the triggering event definition**: Align both requirements on a shared understanding of what “rescinded” means in context—e.g., distinguish between user-initiated rescind vs. system-initiated cancellation. Then assign distinct behaviors accordingly (e.g., user rescind → manual override; system rescind due to flight plan resumption → continue to waypoint). This eliminates ambiguity in the triggering event itself, which is central to Event Conflicts.

Original_FR: If a flight route is assigned to a UAV which is currently hovering in place, the FlightManager shall assign the route and maintain the hover in place mode.
Conflicting_FR: When a flight route is assigned, the UAV shall immediately exit hover mode and follow the assigned route.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a priority-based mode transition rule**: Modify Requirement A to specify that hover-in-place mode is maintained *only if* the newly assigned route is of “low priority” (e.g., informational or standby). Otherwise, for standard operational routes, Requirement B takes precedence—immediately exiting hover and executing the route. This resolves the conflict by defining clear conditions under which each behavior applies.

2. **Refine timing semantics with a configurable delay**: Update both requirements to include a short, system-configurable transition window (e.g., 1–2 seconds) after route assignment during which the UAV may finalize hover exit procedures before engaging the route. Requirement A would then read: “...maintain hover mode until the route execution start signal is issued,” and Requirement B would clarify: “...shall exit hover mode and begin following the route within [X] seconds of assignment.” This aligns operational sequencing without contradictory immediate actions.

3. **Distinguish between route types in the requirements**: Classify flight routes into categories (e.g., “emergency,” “scheduled,” “preview”) and specify behavior per category. For example, Requirement A applies only to “preview” or “non-executable” routes (used for planning), while Requirement B applies to “executable” routes. This eliminates overlap by scoping each requirement to mutually exclusive operational contexts.

Original_FR: If a lead is scored to the high range the system will supply the lead data and score to the enterprise system.
Conflicting_FR: High-range lead data shall not be transmitted to the enterprise system for confidentiality reasons.
Resolution:
1. **Refine the data transmission scope in Requirement A**: Modify Requirement A to specify that only non-sensitive lead attributes (e.g., score, anonymized identifiers, or aggregated metadata) are transmitted to the enterprise system, while personally identifiable or confidential fields are excluded. This aligns with Requirement B’s confidentiality restriction while preserving the intent of sharing actionable scoring information.

2. **Introduce a conditional transmission rule**: Add a condition to Requirement A stating that high-range lead data is transmitted only if it has been pre-approved for sharing (e.g., via user consent, data classification tag, or encryption compliance). This creates a controlled exception that respects the restriction in Requirement B while enabling necessary data flow under secure conditions.

3. **Clarify “lead data” definition through a shared data taxonomy**: Establish a formal data dictionary that distinguishes between “core lead data” (which may be restricted) and “lead score metadata” (which may be permissible). Update both requirements to reference this taxonomy, ensuring Requirement A refers only to permissible elements and Requirement B explicitly lists prohibited fields—resolving ambiguity at the specification level.

Original_FR: If a question is not understood by our API, the system will send a text containing an example question after the text with the API response.
Conflicting_FR: If a question is not understood, the system shall not provide any example question in the response.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a user preference flag**: Modify both requirements to conditionally trigger the inclusion of an example question based on a configurable user setting (e.g., “show_examples_on_error”). Requirement A applies when the flag is enabled; Requirement B applies when it is disabled. This resolves the conflict by making the output behavior context-dependent rather than absolute.

2. **Refine the error classification**: Clarify that “not understood” encompasses distinct subtypes (e.g., syntactic ambiguity vs. out-of-domain query). Specify that example questions are only provided for certain subtypes (e.g., malformed input per Requirement A), while no examples are given for others (e.g., unsupported topics per Requirement B). This eliminates the conflict by scoping each requirement to non-overlapping conditions.

3. **Separate response channels**: Define that the primary API response never includes example questions (satisfying Requirement B), but an auxiliary channel (e.g., logs, developer console, or optional metadata field) may include examples for debugging or guidance (satisfying the intent of Requirement A without violating B’s output constraint).

Original_FR: If a question is not understood by our API, the system will send a text containing an example question after the text with the API response.
Conflicting_FR: If a question is not understood, the system shall not provide any example question.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a user preference flag**: Modify both requirements to conditionally trigger the example question based on a user-configurable setting (e.g., “show_examples_on_error”). Requirement A would apply when the flag is enabled; Requirement B would apply when it is disabled. This resolves the conflict by making the output behavior context-dependent rather than absolute.

2. **Differentiate error severity or type**: Clarify that Requirement A applies only to ambiguous or malformed questions (where an example could aid understanding), while Requirement B applies to questions that are understood but outside the system’s domain or policy (where examples could be misleading). This partitions the output behavior based on the nature of the “not understood” condition.

3. **Replace explicit example with generic guidance**: Revise Requirement A to remove the specific “example question” and instead provide a generic, non-example-based prompt (e.g., “Please rephrase your question in the form: ‘What is…?’”). This satisfies the intent of aiding the user without violating Requirement B’s prohibition on providing example questions.

Original_FR: If LO < = T < = UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Conflicting_FR: If LO < = T <  UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Resolution:
1. **Harmonize the boundary condition by explicitly defining inclusion of the upper bound**: Modify Requirement B to match Requirement A (i.e., change “T < UO” to “T ≤ UO”) if system behavior at T = UO is intended to trigger the temperature status output. This eliminates ambiguity and ensures consistent event triggering at the boundary.

2. **Clarify distinct behaviors at the upper bound and split the requirements accordingly**: If T = UO should *not* trigger the output (as implied by Requirement B), revise Requirement A to exclude the upper bound (i.e., “LO ≤ T < UO”) and add a separate requirement specifying the system’s behavior exactly at T = UO (e.g., output a different status or take no action). This resolves the conflict by decoupling overlapping event conditions.

3. **Introduce a priority or precedence rule for overlapping events**: Keep both requirements as-is but specify that when T = UO, only one of the conditions applies—e.g., define that the stricter condition (Requirement B) takes precedence, or explicitly state that Requirement A governs the inclusive case. This coordination avoids contradictory triggers without altering the logical expressions themselves.

Original_FR: If LO < = T < = UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Conflicting_FR: The temperature status shall be output no more than once per second, even if T fluctuates rapidly within limits.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a debouncing mechanism with a one-second cooldown**: Modify Requirement A to specify that the temperature status is only output when the condition LO ≤ T ≤ UO is first satisfied *and* at least one second has elapsed since the last output. This aligns the event-triggered behavior of Requirement A with the rate restriction in Requirement B by ensuring outputs are spaced at least one second apart, even during sustained or oscillating in-range conditions.

2. **Clarify that Requirement A triggers eligibility, not immediate emission**: Reframe Requirement A to state that the system *becomes eligible* to output the temperature status when LO ≤ T ≤ UO, but actual output is governed by Requirement B’s one-second throttling rule. This decouples the logical condition from the physical output event, resolving the conflict by treating Requirement A as a precondition and Requirement B as the output scheduler.

3. **Add a state-based output rule**: Specify that once the temperature enters the valid range (LO ≤ T ≤ UO), the system outputs the status immediately (per Requirement A), but suppresses any further outputs—even if T exits and re-enters the range—until one second has passed since the last output. This satisfies both requirements by honoring the event condition while enforcing the temporal restriction through stateful tracking.

Original_FR: If T < LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Conflicting_FR: If T > LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Resolution:
1. **Harmonize the logical condition for temperature limit exceedance**: Replace both requirements with a single, unambiguous condition: “If T < LO or T > UO, then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.” This resolves the event conflict by ensuring consistent triggering logic—Requirement A uses T < LO while Requirement B incorrectly uses T > LO (which overlaps with normal operation); the corrected form aligns with standard upper/lower bound violation semantics.

2. **Clarify the intended meaning of LO and UO in each requirement**: If LO and UO represent distinct thresholds in different operational modes (e.g., different subsystems or states), explicitly qualify each requirement with its context (e.g., “During cooling mode…” or “For reactor core monitoring…”). This prevents overlapping or contradictory event triggers by scoping each condition to its relevant domain.

3. **Introduce a unified temperature monitoring event specification**: Define a single system-level event “TemperatureLimitExceeded” triggered exclusively when T < LO or T > UO, and reference this event in both requirements instead of duplicating the logic. This eliminates redundancy and ensures consistent event detection across the system.

Original_FR: If T < LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Conflicting_FR: The THEMAS system shall recognize temperature limit exceedance only if it persists for more than 3 consecutive seconds, preventing false alerts.
Resolution:
1. **Refine Requirement A to incorporate the persistence condition from Requirement B**: Modify Requirement A to state: “If T < LO or UO < T for more than 3 consecutive seconds, then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.” This aligns the event trigger (temperature out-of-bound) with the restriction (minimum duration), resolving the conflict by making the recognition conditional on both the event and its persistence.

2. **Introduce a two-stage recognition mechanism**: Keep both requirements but clarify their roles—Requirement A defines the *detection* of an out-of-limit event (immediate sensing), while Requirement B governs the *alerting* logic (only after 3 seconds of persistence). Explicitly specify that “detection” and “recognition for alerting” are distinct system behaviors, thereby reconciling the event definition with the restriction on action.

3. **Define “recognize” consistently across both requirements**: Add a glossary entry or specification note stating that “recognize” in the context of limit exceedance inherently includes validation of persistence (≥3 seconds). Apply this definition uniformly so that Requirement A’s “recognize” is interpreted under the constraint of Requirement B, eliminating ambiguity and ensuring coherent interpretation.

Original_FR: If that thermostat still needs a heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Conflicting_FR: If that thermostat requires no more heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify the triggering condition by defining mutually exclusive thermostat states**: Replace the ambiguous phrases “still needs” and “requires no more” with precise, non-overlapping operational states (e.g., “thermostat setpoint not yet reached” vs. “thermostat setpoint satisfied”). This ensures that only one requirement triggers at a time based on a well-defined system state.

2. **Introduce a priority or precedence rule for event handling**: Specify that Requirement A takes precedence when the thermostat is in an active heating/cooling demand state, while Requirement B is either removed or rephrased to handle a different scenario (e.g., turning the unit *off* when no longer needed). This eliminates contradictory actions triggered by logically opposite events.

3. **Reconcile the requirements by correcting the intended action in Requirement B**: Given the likely typo or logic error, revise Requirement B to state: “If that thermostat requires no more heating or cooling, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit **off**.” This aligns the event (no further need) with the appropriate action, resolving the conflict by ensuring complementary rather than contradictory behavior.

Original_FR: If that thermostat still needs a heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall submit the request only once per thermostat per heating/cooling cycle, and within 1 second of detecting the need.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify the triggering event and enforce idempotency**: Modify Requirement A to specify that the request is submitted only upon the *initial detection* of a need for heating or cooling (i.e., when the thermostat transitions from “no action needed” to “action needed”). This aligns with Requirement B’s “once per cycle” constraint by tying the event to a state change rather than a continuous condition, eliminating repeated submissions while preserving responsiveness.

2. **Introduce a cooldown or cycle-tracking mechanism**: Augment the system specification with an explicit state variable (e.g., `requestSubmittedForCurrentCycle`) per thermostat. Requirement A should be refined to check this flag before submitting a request, and Requirement B’s “once per cycle” rule should define a “cycle” as the interval from when heating/cooling is first needed until the setpoint is satisfied and the unit turns off. This ensures the event in A only triggers once per defined cycle.

3. **Synchronize timing and submission logic**: Refine both requirements to share a common event handler that activates within 1 second of need detection (per Requirement B) and guarantees exactly one request submission per cycle. Specifically, reword Requirement A to: “Upon detecting a need for heating or cooling, and only if no request has been submitted for the current cycle, the process shall submit a request within 1 second.” This merges the intent of both requirements into a single, unambiguous directive.

Original_FR: If the business rules allow this transaction is to be aggregated the PSAM must determine if it has an aggregation record for the card issuer. If the PSAM does not have an aggregation record for the card issuer a new aggregation record for the card issuer must be created
Conflicting_FR: The PSAM shall not create new aggregation records under any circumstances; missing records shall be ignored.
Resolution:
1. **Modify Requirement A to defer aggregation record creation to an external system**: Revise Requirement A so that if the PSAM does not have an aggregation record for the card issuer, it logs the need for a new record and signals an external provisioning system to create it—thereby complying with Requirement B’s prohibition on the PSAM itself creating records.

2. **Clarify scope of “under any circumstances” in Requirement B**: Amend Requirement B to explicitly allow record creation when mandated by business rules (as in Requirement A), changing it to: “The PSAM shall not create new aggregation records except when required by active business rules for transaction aggregation.” This aligns both requirements under a defined exception.

3. **Introduce a pre-validation step before transaction processing**: Require that all eligible card issuers have pre-provisioned aggregation records before any transaction is processed. Update system procedures so that Requirement A only checks for existing records (without creating them), and Requirement B remains intact—eliminating the operational conflict by design.

Original_FR: If the CND contains an ISIM/UICC it shall support the AKA authentication method (TS 187 001 [8])
Conflicting_FR: If the CND contains an ISIM/UICC, it shall not support the AKA authentication method and shall use HTTP Digest instead.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify Applicability Scope by Authentication Context**: Modify both requirements to specify distinct operational contexts (e.g., based on network type or service domain). For example, require AKA support for 3GPP-based services (per TS 187 001) while mandating HTTP Digest for non-3GPP or IMS-specific applications. This resolves the restriction conflict by partitioning the conditions under which each authentication method applies.

2. **Introduce a Priority or Precedence Rule**: Explicitly state that Requirement A takes precedence over Requirement B when both apply, citing the normative reference to TS 187 001 as a standards-compliance obligation. Update Requirement B to read: “...shall use HTTP Digest instead, unless AKA is mandated by an applicable 3GPP standard such as TS 187 001.”

3. **Refactor into a Single Conditional Requirement**: Replace both conflicting statements with a unified requirement that selects the authentication method based on a well-defined system parameter (e.g., network registration type). Example: “If the CND contains an ISIM/UICC, it shall support AKA when connecting to a 3GPP network and HTTP Digest when authenticating to an IMS application over non-3GPP access, in accordance with TS 187 001.”

Original_FR: If the CND does not contain an ISIM/UICC it shall support the HTTP Digest authentication or the NBA
Conflicting_FR: If the CND does not contain an ISIM/UICC, it shall not support HTTP Digest authentication or the NBA.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a conditional priority or context**: Modify both requirements to specify mutually exclusive operational contexts (e.g., based on deployment scenario, regulatory domain, or user profile). For example: “If the CND does not contain an ISIM/UICC and is deployed in Region X, it shall support HTTP Digest authentication or NBA; if deployed in Region Y, it shall not support them.” This resolves the conflict by scoping each requirement to non-overlapping conditions.

2. **Replace the conflicting mandates with a single unambiguous requirement**: Reconcile the two by selecting one behavior based on architectural or security policy decisions. For instance: “If the CND does not contain an ISIM/UICC, it shall support HTTP Digest authentication but shall not support NBA” (or vice versa), thereby eliminating contradictory obligations and aligning with system design goals.

3. **Clarify the intended meaning of ‘support’**: The conflict may stem from ambiguous terminology. Define “support” precisely—e.g., distinguish between mandatory implementation, optional capability, or runtime enablement. Then revise one requirement accordingly: e.g., Requirement A could mandate that the CND *be capable of* HTTP Digest/NBA, while Requirement B mandates that these methods *must be disabled by default*. This resolves the operational contradiction through refined semantics.

Original_FR: If the DPU-SCUI FIFO is full when DPU-DPA attempts to enqueue its data product, DPU-DPA shall suspend event processing until room is available on the respective FIFO for the current data product. Incoming data may be dropped while suspended. An error shall be reported DPU-CCM.
Conflicting_FR: DPU-DPA shall continue processing events even if the FIFO is full, potentially overwriting incoming data.
Resolution:
1. **Adopt a priority-based queuing policy**: Modify both requirements to specify that DPU-DPA shall continue processing high-priority events even when the FIFO is full (aligning with Requirement B), but shall suspend processing of low-priority events and report an error to DPU-CCM in those cases (aligning with Requirement A). This resolves the conflict by differentiating behavior based on event priority, making the operation conditional rather than contradictory.

2. **Introduce a configurable overflow mode**: Update the system specification to allow DPU-DPA to operate in one of two mutually exclusive modes: (a) *blocking mode* (per Requirement A), where event processing halts until FIFO space is available, or (b) *overwrite mode* (per Requirement B), where newer data may overwrite older unprocessed data. The active mode shall be set via a configuration parameter managed by DPU-CCM, ensuring consistent behavior at runtime.

3. **Define a hybrid FIFO management strategy**: Revise both requirements to state that DPU-DPA shall attempt to enqueue data; if the FIFO is full, it shall first drop the oldest enqueued low-criticality data product (if any) to make room, and only suspend processing if no such data exists. This combines controlled overwriting (from B) with suspension as a last resort (from A), eliminating the operational contradiction while preserving data integrity for critical products.

Original_FR: if the item the user wants is available for exchange, the user can request the exchange of items that he/she has with the item he/she wants.
Conflicting_FR: Users shall not be allowed to request exchanges even if items are available.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a conditional exception to Requirement B**: Modify Requirement B to state, “Users shall not be allowed to request exchanges even if items are available, *except for users who meet specific eligibility criteria* (e.g., verified accounts, sufficient reputation score, or completed onboarding).” This preserves the restriction while allowing controlled fulfillment of Requirement A under defined conditions.

2. **Refine scope by user role or item category**: Clarify that Requirement A applies only to certain user roles (e.g., premium members) or specific item categories (e.g., non-restricted goods), while Requirement B applies universally otherwise. This resolves the conflict by partitioning the domain so both requirements operate in non-overlapping contexts.

3. **Add a system-level approval step**: Keep both requirements but insert a mediation rule: “Exchange requests may be initiated per Requirement A, but are automatically blocked per Requirement B unless explicitly approved by an administrator or automated policy engine.” This maintains the restriction as a default while enabling exceptions through a controlled workflow.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the high average score then it will be process by the ARA functionality.
Conflicting_FR: Leads with high average score shall be ignored by the ARA functionality.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify and align the operational condition**: Reconcile the contradictory actions by explicitly defining that leads with a "high average score" are *not* processed by ARA. Update Requirement A to either exclude "high average score" from its trigger condition (e.g., “If the lead score falls within the *low or medium* range…”) or redefine the scoring categories so that “high average” is unambiguously excluded from ARA processing, matching Requirement B.

2. **Introduce a precedence rule or exception clause**: Modify the requirements to include an explicit exception: “ARA shall process leads based on score thresholds, *except* those classified as high average, which shall be ignored.” This preserves the general logic of Requirement A while incorporating the specific exclusion in Requirement B, resolving the operational contradiction.

3. **Refine terminology and score classification**: Eliminate ambiguity by formally defining score ranges (e.g., low: 0–49, medium: 50–79, high average: 80–89, high: 90–100) in a shared glossary, and ensure both requirements reference these precise definitions. Then adjust Requirement A to specify it applies only to non–high-average scores, ensuring consistent interpretation and eliminating the operational conflict.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the low average then it will be returned to the supplying vendor
Conflicting_FR: Leads with low average score shall instead be processed by ARA functionality.
Resolution:
1. **Define a precedence rule**: Explicitly specify that ARA functionality takes precedence over vendor return for leads with a low average score. Update Requirement A to read: “If the lead score falls within the low average range and ARA processing is not enabled or applicable, it will be returned to the supplying vendor.” This clarifies the operational sequence and eliminates ambiguity.

2. **Introduce a conditional routing criterion**: Modify both requirements to include a mutually exclusive condition (e.g., based on lead source, campaign type, or vendor contract). For example: “Leads with a low average score from Vendor X shall be returned to the vendor; all other low-average-score leads shall be processed by ARA functionality.” This partitions the operational scope to avoid overlap.

3. **Implement a two-stage handling workflow**: Revise the system behavior so that low-average-score leads are first processed by ARA functionality, and only if ARA determines the lead is unqualified or non-actionable, then it is returned to the vendor. Update both requirements to reflect this sequential operation, ensuring they describe different steps in a unified process rather than competing actions.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the medium average then it will be set for manual verification by an Enrollment Coordinator through the eleads system.
Conflicting_FR: Leads with medium average score shall be automatically approved without verification.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify the definition of “medium average score” by subdividing it into distinct sub-ranges**: Modify both requirements to specify that “medium-low” scores trigger manual verification (per Requirement A), while “medium-high” scores are auto-approved (per Requirement B). This eliminates the operational contradiction by ensuring each lead falls under only one handling rule.

2. **Introduce a configurable business rule with priority logic**: Update the system specification to treat Requirement B as the default behavior (auto-approval for medium scores) but allow Requirement A to override it when specific conditions apply (e.g., lead source, region, or compliance flag). Document this precedence explicitly in the requirements to resolve the operational conflict.

3. **Align both requirements through stakeholder reconciliation**: Facilitate a decision session with Enrollment Coordinators and automation owners to agree on a single operational path—either manual verification or auto-approval—for all medium-average leads, then revise one requirement to match the agreed policy, removing the conflicting instruction.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: Additional cooling requests shall be rejected instead of being queued.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify the operational context and define distinct modes**: Specify that Requirement A applies during normal or high-load operational modes (where queuing is acceptable), while Requirement B applies during emergency or critical overload conditions (where immediate rejection prevents system instability). This resolves the conflict by scoping each requirement to a mutually exclusive operational state.

2. **Introduce a configurable policy parameter**: Replace the hard-coded behaviors in both requirements with a system-configurable policy (e.g., “cooling_request_handling_policy”) that can be set to either “queue_lifo” or “reject_immediately.” This allows the system to comply with one requirement at a time based on deployment needs, eliminating the operational conflict through runtime or configuration-time selection.

3. **Refine the trigger condition for queuing vs. rejection**: Modify Requirement A to queue requests only if the system has spare capacity to manage the queue (e.g., memory, expected cooldown window), and align Requirement B to activate only when queuing would risk violating safety or performance thresholds. This creates a conditional handoff between the two behaviors based on measurable system states, resolving the conflict through precise boundary conditions.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: Requests shall be queued only if the maximum number of units is not exceeded.
Resolution:
1. **Reconcile queuing conditions by aligning thresholds**: Modify Requirement B to clarify that queuing occurs *only when* the system has reached the maximum number of concurrently running units—i.e., “Requests shall be queued if and only if the maximum number of units is already running.” This directly aligns with Requirement A’s trigger condition and eliminates the operational contradiction.

2. **Standardize queue policy and explicitly define its activation**: Update both requirements to reference a shared operational rule: “When the number of active heating or cooling units equals the configured maximum, new requests are placed in a LIFO queue.” This ensures consistency in both the condition for queuing (from B) and the queuing behavior (from A).

3. **Introduce a system state variable to govern queuing logic**: Define a clear system state (e.g., “UnitsAtMaxCapacity”) that activates queuing. Both requirements should reference this state: Requirement A specifies the LIFO action when the state is true, and Requirement B is rephrased to say queuing occurs *exclusively* when this state is active. This decouples intent from implementation and resolves ambiguity in operation triggers.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: If the maximum number of  cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Resolution:
1. **Harmonize scope by aligning Requirement B with Requirement A**: Modify Requirement B to explicitly state that it is a subset of Requirement A, e.g., “If the maximum number of cooling units is already running (as part of the overall limit on heating or cooling units), the request will be added to a LIFO queue.” This eliminates ambiguity by clarifying that cooling units fall under the broader category in Requirement A, ensuring consistent event handling.

2. **Define a unified queuing policy for all unit types**: Replace both requirements with a single, consolidated requirement: “If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, any new request—regardless of unit type—will be added to a shared LIFO queue.” This resolves the event conflict by ensuring only one triggering condition and one response action governs the queuing behavior.

3. **Specify distinct queues per unit type with explicit precedence**: If separate handling is intentional, revise the requirements to clarify that heating and cooling each have their own LIFO queues, and define which event takes precedence when the total system capacity is reached (e.g., “Cooling requests are queued in a dedicated LIFO queue; if total active units—cooling plus heating—reach the system maximum, further requests of either type are rejected or queued per their respective policies”). This makes the event conditions and outcomes unambiguous and non-overlapping.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: The LIFO queue shall allow no more than 5 pending requests, rejecting additional requests until previous ones are processed.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify queue behavior under full capacity in Requirement A**: Modify Requirement A to explicitly state that new requests are only added to the LIFO queue if the queue has fewer than 5 pending requests; otherwise, they are rejected. This aligns the enqueuing logic with the restriction in Requirement B and eliminates ambiguity about what happens when both the units and the queue are at capacity.

2. **Introduce a unified overflow policy**: Define a system-level rule that governs request handling when both the maximum number of units is running *and* the LIFO queue is full (i.e., 5 pending requests). For example: “If the queue is full, new requests shall be rejected with a ‘queue overflow’ status.” This resolves the event conflict by specifying a deterministic outcome for the overlapping condition described in both requirements.

3. **Synchronize event triggers between requirements**: Specify that the “request rejection” event in Requirement B takes precedence over the “add to queue” event in Requirement A when the queue is at its 5-request limit. This ensures that the restriction (max 5 pending) actively constrains the event (enqueuing), directly addressing the Event–Restriction conflict by defining priority in event handling.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is not running, this process will generate a request to turn on the requested unit.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall never generate a request to turn on a unit, even if the maximum is not reached.
Resolution:
1. **Reconcile activation conditions by refining Requirement A**: Modify Requirement A to include an explicit exception clause that aligns with Requirement B—for example: “If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is not running *and system safety or operational policies permit activation*, this process will generate a request to turn on the requested unit.” This preserves the intent of A while respecting the absolute prohibition in B under specific circumstances.

2. **Introduce a priority-based rule hierarchy**: Define a clear precedence rule stating that Requirement B overrides Requirement A in all cases. Update the system specification to reflect that the prohibition in B is a hard constraint, and any logic implementing A must first check compliance with B before generating a unit activation request.

3. **Clarify scope or context boundaries for each requirement**: Specify distinct operational contexts for each requirement—e.g., Requirement A applies during normal operation mode, while Requirement B applies during maintenance, emergency shutdown, or security lockdown modes. This eliminates the conflict by ensuring the two requirements are never simultaneously active.

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select a few events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall select all events and ignore any limit on the number of events in the report.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce context-dependent selection rules**: Modify both requirements to specify that the “few events” in Requirement A apply only to the operational history report, while Requirement B’s “all events” rule applies to other report types (e.g., audit or compliance reports). This resolves the restriction conflict by scoping each requirement to distinct use cases.

2. **Define a configurable event limit with override capability**: Update Requirement A to state that the number of events selected for the operational history report is configurable (e.g., via a system parameter), and revise Requirement B to clarify that “ignore any limit” applies only when an explicit “full export” mode is activated—thus allowing both behaviors under controlled conditions.

3. **Clarify precedence based on user role or request type**: Specify that Requirement B takes precedence when the request originates from an authorized auditor or system administrator, while Requirement A applies to standard user requests. This resolves the conflict by introducing a conditional rule tied to requester authority or intent.

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select all the events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select a few events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Resolution:
1. **Clarify the scope of “all” vs. “a few” by defining explicit filtering criteria**: Replace the ambiguous phrase “a few events” in Requirement B with a precise condition (e.g., “events matching user-specified filters” or “events from the last 30 days”), while retaining “all events” in Requirement A for an unfiltered report. This resolves the input conflict by distinguishing two distinct report modes based on user input or context.

2. **Introduce a parameterized input to control event selection**: Modify both requirements to share a common conditional structure: “If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select events from the event database according to the user-specified scope (‘full’ or ‘filtered’), sort the selected events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.” This eliminates the conflict by making the input (selection scope) explicit and configurable.

3. **Differentiate the requirements by report subtype**: Rename or qualify the reports to reflect their differing data scopes—e.g., “full operational history report” (Requirement A) and “summary operational history report” (Requirement B)—and specify that each is triggered by a distinct user selection. This ensures the inputs (report type selections) are mutually exclusive, removing ambiguity at the input level.

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select all the events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall only select a single event for the operational history report.
Resolution:
1. **Refine Requirement A to apply only to a specific report type**: Modify Requirement A to explicitly state that it applies to a “comprehensive operational history report,” while Requirement B governs a “single-event operational history report.” This creates two distinct report options, eliminating the restriction conflict by scoping each requirement to a different user-selected mode.

2. **Introduce a user-configurable selection criterion in Requirement A**: Update Requirement A to include a conditional clause—e.g., “If the operational history report is selected *and no specific event is specified*, THEMAS shall select all events…”—while keeping Requirement B as the behavior when a single event is explicitly chosen. This resolves the conflict by making the scope of data selection dependent on user input.

3. **Reconcile via priority or default behavior**: Specify that Requirement B defines the default behavior (single-event report), and Requirement A describes an optional extended mode that must be explicitly enabled (e.g., via a “full history” flag). Document this interaction in the system specification to clarify that both requirements coexist under controlled conditions, removing ambiguity and restriction overlap.

Original_FR: If the POS device supports multiple applications or multiple transaction types an interaction between the terminal and consumer or sales agent determines the CEP application and the function to be performed (cancel last purchase transaction)
Conflicting_FR: POS device shall always use the default application regardless of user interaction.
Resolution:
1. **Introduce a configurable default behavior with override capability**: Modify Requirement B to state that the POS device shall use the default application *unless* the device supports multiple applications and explicit user interaction (as described in Requirement A) selects an alternative. This preserves the default behavior while allowing overrides when multi-application support is active, resolving the operational contradiction.
